title: Multigenerational Households (three or more generations under one roof)
survey: 2020 Census / ACS table B11017 concept, as published by the U.S. Census Bureau
units: count of households; share of households; share of children (derived by Census)

provenance.source: U.S. Census Bureau, 'Several Generations Under One Roof' (America Counts story, June 2023), reporting 2020 Census multigenerational-household figures; the tabulation concept is ACS table B11017 (Multigenerational Households).
provenance.fetch_method: WebFetch of the Census Bureau America Counts story page (the exact B11017 API counts require a Census API key and data.census.gov is a JS SPA; the Census Bureau's own published figures in the story are used, which are rounded to the tenth of a percent / to the hundred-thousand household).
provenance.definition: A multigenerational household has THREE OR MORE generations living together (per the Census story wording). This matches the gate-2b B11017 rule after fixes B/C (>= 3 distinct lineal generations present); the span>=2 predecessor the round-1 referee rejected would NOT be concept-matched to B11017.
provenance.note: These are published, rounded Census figures for 2020 (a decennial-era benchmark). The PSID gate estimand is effectively 1997-2023, so the anchor carries an era delta in addition to the unit (household-vs-person) and family-unit-vs-household deltas. Reported, never a level target.

concept_note: The gate-2b multigen cells are a PERSON-LEVEL share (share of persons whose family unit spans >= 3 lineal generations), by age x sex. The 4.7% here is a HOUSEHOLD-level share. Persons-in-multigen-households exceed the multigen-household share (multigen households are larger), so the person-level PSID rate sits above 4.7% by construction; the anchor names the household-vs-person and family-unit-vs-household deltas rather than differencing the levels. The children-in-grandparents'-home figure (8.4%) is a grandchild-side person share and anchors the DIRECTION of the coresident_grandchild family, whose PSID cells are grandparent-side (a different denominator, named).
